0.title: About Me
0.content: I am Oluwasegun, a PhD candidate in Artificial Intelligence and Electrical & Computer Engineering at Oregon State University, where I study stochastic gradient learning. Stochastic gradient learning can be considered as one of the most important algorithms in the world, behind the success of deep learning. As an early-career researcher, I am currently studying why training deep neural networks via the stochastic gradient algorithm succeeds in practice. While classic optimization theory motivates, they do not provide exact fundamental guarantees for the deep learning setting. My current research shows that a key factor is the presence of fundamental signal processing elements found in almost all practical stochastic gradient learning variants. Long‑term vision: . My long-term goal is to establish myself as a leader in the development and formalization of the learning algorithms that drive artificial intelligence. I am deeply motivated by the challenge of uncovering and closing the gap on the fundamental dynamics that underpin successful learning algorithms, particularly those powering deep learning. Aiming to deepen our understanding of why certain algorithms succeed and how they can be improved, I seek to advance research that bridges theoretical insights with practical performance. Through collaboration and innovation, I look to develop new approaches that are both theoretically grounded and reliably effective in solving complex, real-world problems. 📬 Let’s connect: If you’re interested in research collaboration, applied AI projects, feel free to reach out via LinkedIn or Email.

1.title: AutoSGM
1.content: Connecting the dots … HB, NAG, Adam. Preliminary versions of this work was presented at ICASSP 2024. Page created: Sep 11 2025 at 12:00 AM . | AutoSGM . | 🌀 The Core Update Rule | 🧩 Unifying PHB, NAG, and Adam | 🎯 Lowpass Regularization | 📊 Some Empirical Results . | 1. VIT on CIFAR10. | 2. ResNet18 on CIFAR10. | 3. GPT-2 on Shakespeare-char: | 4. GPT-2 on WikiText-103. | . | 🏁 Conclusion | . | . Momentum-based stochastic gradient methods such as Polyak’s Heavy Ball (PHB), Nesterov’s Accelerated Gradient (NAG), and Adam dominate deep learning optimization. They are often treated as separate algorithms, but in our recent work, we show they are all special cases of a single signal-processing (DSP) structure. The framework that allows us to do this is called the Automatic Stochastic Gradient Method (AutoSGM) framework. AutoSGM reframes these stochastic gradient optimizers through the lens of a first-order lowpass filter applied to the stochastic gradient, and the existence of an optimal iteration-dependent learning rate choice. The AutoSGM framework reveals: . | the first-order filtering mechanics behind what has been called momentum. | that we can derive an optimal, iteration-dependent learning rate choice that involves moment estimation. | that the smoothing effect of the first-order filter is a lowpass regularization of the loss surface. | . All algebraic operations are sample-by-sample (elementwise) unless otherwise stated. The shorthand notation \((t,i)\) denotes the \(i\)-th element of a vector at iteration \(t\).

2.title: 🌀 The Core Update Rule
2.content: The classic stochastic gradient method (SGM) updates parameters as: . \[\mathbf{w}(t+1,i) = \mathbf{w}(t,i) - \alpha(t,i) \, \mathbf{g}(t,i)\] where: . | \(\mathbf{g}(t,i) = \nabla f(\mathbf{w}(t,i))\) is an unbiased stochastic gradient component, | \(\alpha(t,i)\) denotes the learning rate at iteration \(t\), determined via a selected oracle function. | . In AutoSGM, we replace the stochastic gradient with a smoothed version: . \[\mathbf{w}(t+1,i) = \mathbf{w}(t,i) - \alpha(t,i) \, H_{\beta,\gamma}(\mathbf{g}(t,i))\] Here, \(H_{\beta,\gamma}\) is a first-order filter with transfer function: . \[H(z) = \eta \, \frac{1 - \gamma z^{-1}}{1 - \beta z^{-1}}, \quad 0 \le \beta < 1, \ \gamma < \beta\] The time (iteration)-domain realization is: . \[\mathbf{v}(t,i) = \beta\,\mathbf{v}(t-1,i) + \eta\,(\mathbf{g}(t,i) - \gamma\,\mathbf{g}(t-1,i))\] See the learning dynamics of the stochastic gradient update in this framework. Also, see smoothing is not averaging for how the filter called momentum is generally not an exponential moving average, EMA. Using an extremely simple problem setup, we show in Momentum: a principled signal-processing framework that the first-order smoothing filter commonly called momentum is a principled signal processing operation✅, and in particular derive Nesterov’s Accelerated Gradient (NAG) from first principles as a point in the filter design space where we set \(\gamma=\tfrac{\beta}{1+\beta}\).

3.title: 🧩 Unifying PHB, NAG, and Adam
3.content: Choosing \(\beta, \gamma, \alpha(t,i)\) appropriately, the AutoSGM framework recovers . | Algorithm | \(\beta\) | \(\gamma\) | \(\eta\) | \(\alpha(t,i)\) | . | Basic | \(0\) | \(0\) | \(0\) | \(\mu \digamma(t)\) | . | PHB | \(✓\) | \(0\) | \(1\) | \(\mu \digamma(t)\) | . | NAG | \(✓\) | \({\beta}/{(1+\beta)}\) | \((1+\beta)\) | \(\mu \digamma(t)\) | . | Adam | \(✓\) | \(0\) | \(1-\beta\) | \({\mu} \digamma(t) \cdot{\mathbf{d}(t,i)}^{-1}\) | .

4.title: 🎯 Lowpass Regularization
4.content: Incorporating momentum is known to practically help stabilize learning dynamics and avoid shallow local minima (Haykin, 2008). In the paper, we use the impulse response of the filter to show that smoothing the gradient (also called momentum) is approximately equivalent to smoothing the loss surface: . This Lowpass regularization due to smoothing the gradient reflects the stabilized training effect of: . | reduced noise in the gradient updates, | improved convergence to flatter local minima, | . often observed.

5.title: 📊 Some Empirical Results
5.content: Using Adam as a fixed-numerator baseline for the learning rate, we tested the AutoSGM framework using a derived iteration-dependent partial correlation numerator alternative on CIFAR-10 image classification (ViT, ResNet) and language modeling (GPT-2 on WikiText and Shakespeare): . | Tuning the filter’s zero \(\gamma\) improved performance in most cases. | Iteration-dependent learning rate numerator (circled dots) outperformed fixed numerator (squared dots). | . 1. VIT on CIFAR10. | | . 2. ResNet18 on CIFAR10. | | . 3. GPT-2 on Shakespeare-char: . | | . 4. GPT-2 on WikiText-103. | | .

6.title: 🏁 Conclusion
6.content: AutoSGM offers a unified, interpretable, and tunable framework for what has traditionally been referred to as momentum-based optimization. We can operate PHB, NAG, and Adam as points in the AutoSGM parameter space. Overall AutoSGM is a foundational framework for studying stochastic gradient algorithms, enabling systematic separation of filter design, automatic learning-rate function choices and the non-unique implementations present in current methods. 💡 Takeaway: If you have been switching between Adam, NAG, and PHB, you might not need to. They are all part of the same family. AutoSGM gives you the structure or map. | Haykin, S. (2008). Neural Networks and Learning Machines (3rd edition). Pearson. | .

8.title: Stochastic Gradient Learning Dynamics
8.content: Linear Time (Iteration) Varying System. Oluwasegun Somefun. “Smooth Learning Dynamics.” AutoSGM Framework, 2025. Please cite this page if you use information from these notes for your work, research, or anything that requires academic or formal citation. | Stochastic Gradient Learning Dynamics . | Weight decay | A Proximal Subproblem | Stability Conditions and Transient Behavior | . | . The AutoSGM framework exposes the exact update trajectory of each trainable parameter in a gradient-generating system like a deep neural network via the stochastic gradient algorithm under a lowpass filter (momentum) and iteration-dependent learning-rate oracle as the dynamics of a first-order linear time (iteration) varying (LTV) filter. This LTV description makes it possible to apply linear systems, control and signal‑processing tools to reason about stability, transient response, noise attenuation and steady-state convergence tradeoffs. Formally, at each iteration \(t\), a single parameter \(\mathbf{w}[t, i]\) update via its gradient component \(\mathbf{g}[t, i]\) follows the first-order linear filter trajectory . \[\boxed{\state{\Delta\mathbf{w}[t+1, i]} = \filter{\beta}\,\gain{r[t, i]}\cdot\state{\Delta\mathbf{w}[t,i]} + \filter{\eta}\,\gain{\alpha[t,i]}\cdot \input{\mathbf{e}[t,i]}},\] . Weight decay . Depending on the weight-decay mechanism, the generated input trajectory for what has been called decoupled weight-decay . \[\input{ \mathbf{e}[t, i]} = \bigl(\filter{\gamma}\,\statez{\mathbf{g}[t-1, i]} - \statez{\mathbf{g}[t, i]} \bigr) + \gainx{\rho}\,\filter{\eta^{-1}}\bigl(\filter{\beta} \,\statex{\tilde{\mathbf{w}}[t-1, i]} - \statex{\tilde{\mathbf{w}}[t, i]}\bigr),\] is exactly equivalent to the standard weight-decay . \[\input{\mathbf{e}[t, i]} = \bigl(\filter{\gamma}\,\statez{\mathbf{g}[t-1, i]} - \statez{\mathbf{g}[t, i]} \bigr) + \gainx{\rho^{\prime}}\bigl(\filter{\gamma} \,\statex{\mathbf{w}[t-1, i]} - \statex{\mathbf{w}[t, i]} \bigr),\] when \(\gainx{\rho^\prime} = \gainx{\rho}\,\filter{\eta^{-1}}\), \(\filter{\beta}=\filter{\gamma}\), and \(\statex{\tilde{\mathbf{w}}[t, i]} = \statex{\mathbf{w}[t, i]}\). Finally, by integrating the LTV filter, the actual parameter update is recovered . \(\boxed{\statex{\mathbf{w}[t+1, i]} = \statex{\mathbf{w}[t,i]} + \state{\Delta \mathbf{w}[t+1,i]}}\), . where . | \(\gain{\alpha[t,i]} = {\gain{\mu}}\,\gain{\digamma[t}] \cdot \frac{\gain{\mathbf{a}[t,i]}}{\gain{\mathbf{d}[t,i]}}\) is an iteration-dependent learning rate oracle, essentially composed of a trust-region constant \(0 < \gain{\mu} < 1\), a window function \(0 \le \gain{\digamma[t]} \le 1\) as the learning-rate schedule, together with the oracle’s numerator and denominator functions denoted respectively as \(\gain{\mathbf{a}[t,i]}\), \(\gain{\mathbf{d}[t,i]}\). | \(\gain{r[t,i]} = \gain{\alpha[t,i]}/\gain{\alpha[t-1,i]}\) is the learning rate ratio, | \(\filter{\beta}\) is the lowpass filter’s pole parameter selected for stability \(0 \le \filter{\beta} < 1\), | \(\filter{\gamma}\) is the lowpass filter’s zero parameter, selected such that \(\filter{\gamma} < \filter{\beta}\), | \(\filter{\eta}\) is a constant selected as \((1-\filter{\beta})/(1-\filter{\gamma})\) such the steady-state (DC) gain of the lowpass filter is unity, | \(\gainx{\rho, \rho^\prime} \ge 0\) denote small weight-decay constants, can be selected relative to \(\eta\), | \(\statex{\tilde{\mathbf{w}}[t, i]} = \gain{\mathbf{d}[t,i]}\,\statex{\mathbf{w}[t, i]}\) is the scaled parameter via the learning-rate’s denominator. | . Under the mild assumptions of local smoothness of the loss function generating the gradient, and bounded gradient moments, the trajectory input \(\input{\mathbf{e}[t, i]}\) is bounded. A Proximal Subproblem . Any first‑order linear filter like . \[\boxed{\state{\Delta\mathbf{w}[t+1, i]} = \filter{\beta}\,\gain{r[t, i]}\cdot\state{\Delta\mathbf{w}[t,i]} + \filter{\eta}\,\gain{\alpha[t,i]}\cdot \input{\mathbf{e}[t,i]}}\] can be written as the solution of a proximal subproblem. This first‑order linear filter is the explicit closed‑form optimal solution to a regularized weighted least squares objective: that balances the next trajectory step \(\Delta\mathbf{w}[t+1,i]\) between aligned with \(\mathbf{e}[t,i]\), and being close to a weighted version of the previous trajectory step \(\Delta\mathbf{w}[t,i]\) . \[\Delta\mathbf{w}[t+1,i] = \arg\min_{\Delta} Q\bigl(\Delta\bigr)\] \[Q\bigl(\Delta\bigr)= \;-\; \eta\,\Delta \cdot \mathbf{e}[t,i] \;+\; \frac{1}{2\,\alpha[t,i]}\, \Bigl(\Delta - \beta\, r[t,i]\cdot\Delta\mathbf{w}[t,i]\Bigr)^2\] Rewriting as a proximal operator, . \[\Delta\mathbf{w}[t+1,i] = \mathbf{prox}_{\gain{\alpha[t,i]},\:\eta\,\Delta \cdot\mathbf{e}[t,i]}\big(\beta\, r[t,i]\cdot\Delta\mathbf{w}[t,i]\big)\] Despite the underlying deep learning loss being non‑convex, this per-iteration subproblem is strongly convex in \(\Delta\mathbf{w}[t,i]\) with modulus \(\alpha[t,i]^{-1}\), and driven by \(\mathbf{e}[t,i]\). Apart from unification, this LTV filter and its resulting convex quadratic subproblem formulation, concretely highlights . | Lowpass regularization: The smooth path of parameter changes empirically enhances the robustness and generalization of solutions. | Stability: Each parameter-change is the solution of a strongly convex quadratic subproblem. Provided the learning rate at each iteration is finite, this lowpass filter ensures bounded, well‑defined and stable parameter changes. | . This makes the design of such practical stochastic graient learning algorithms, more principled, analyzable, and unifying across several methods.

9.title: Stability Conditions and Transient Behavior
9.content: As stated, the overall filtered SGM dynamics is \(\boxed{\state{\Delta\mathbf{w}[t+1, i]} = \filter{\beta}\,\gain{r[t, i]}\cdot\state{\Delta\mathbf{w}[t,i]} + \filter{\eta}\,\gain{\alpha[t,i]}\cdot \input{\mathbf{e}[t,i]}}\) . | The lowpass pole \(\filter{\beta}\) and learning-rate ratio \(r[t,i]\) shape both the the low-frequency properties of the filter (response to slow-changing inputs), and exponential stability margin of the system \(\lvert \filter{\beta^{-1}}\gain{ r[t,i]} \rvert < 1\). | . Together, \(\filter{\beta}\) and \(\gain{\alpha[t,i]}\) shape how quickly and smoothly the learning dynamics settle into steady-state. If selected properly, they ensure bounded and convergent behavior over time. For BIBO stability, and uniform exponential stability (boundedness and asymptotic behavior of the trajectory solution): the necessary and sufficient conditions are: \(0 < \filter{\beta} < 1\), and \(\sup_t \gain{\alpha[t,i]} < \infty\). Ensures a bounded input \(\input{\mathbf{e}[t,i]}\) leads to a bounded trajectory output \(\state{\Delta\mathbf{w}[t+1, i]}\). In addition, if \(0 < \filter{\beta} < 1\) to improve exponential stability, we need \(\gain{\digamma[t]} \to 0\) as \(t \to \infty\), hich imply both \(\gain{r[t]} \to 0\) and \(\gain{\alpha[t]} \to 0\). This explains why learning-rate annealing is important.

11.title: Optimal Learning Rate Functions
11.content: Minimizing Trust-region Subproblems. Page created: Sep 11 2025 at 12:00 AM . Oluwasegun Somefun. “AutoSGM: Trust-region Optimal Learning rates.” AutoSGM Framework, 2025. Please cite this page if you use information from these notes for your work, research, or anything that requires academic or formal citation. | Optimal Learning Rate Functions . | Notes | Optimal Iteration-Dependent Learning Rate Oracles . | Per-coordinate Setup | Matrix Setup | with Smoothed Gradients . | Per-coordinate Setup | Matrix Setup | Matrix Inverse Square-root (Realizations) . | Full Eigenvalue Decomposition (EVD) | Newton-Schulz Iteration | . | . | . | . | . AutoSGM reframes stochastic gradient algorithms used in deep learning optimization through the lens of a first-order lowpass filter applied to a stochastic gradient, and the existence of an optimal iteration-dependent learning rate choice. We show here that the automatic learning rate perspective in the AutoSGM framework captures several stochastic gradient learning variants that have been viewed as preconditioning methods.

12.title: Notes
12.content: We assume both the objective function \(f\) and its gradient are Lipschitz continuous (Bottou et al., 2018). For mathematical convenience, to derive optimal learning rates, we restrict the objective function to a log-likelihood objective function \(f=\ln p(\mathbf{w})\). Let \(\mathbb{E}\) denote expectation with respect to a model distribution \(p(\mathbf{w})\). The expected gradient of this function is zero w.r.t \(\mathbf{w}\) (Moon & Stirling, 2000; Van Trees et al., 2013). In practice, many widely used training objectives admit log‑likelihood interpretations but differ from this simplified model. Define \(\mathbf{\varepsilon}[t] = \mathbf{w}[t] - {\mathbf{w}}^\star\) as the parameter error, the gap between current weight and a local optimum. In practice, we can realize the expectations in the derived learning rate functions using time-averages via exponential moving averages (EMA) with long-term memory (Diniz, 2020; Haykin, 2014).

15.doc: A True AutoSGM: Conjugate Gradient Method
15.title: A True AutoSGM: Conjugate Gradient Method
15.content: Solving a Linear System. Sep 11 2025 at 12:00 AM . Please cite this page if you use information from these notes for your work, research, or anything that requires academic or formal citation. Oluwasegun Somefun. “A True AutoSGM: Conjugate Gradient Method.” 2025. The lowpass regularized stochastic gradient algorithm applies a first-order lowpass filter to its stochastic gradient, before multiplying by a possibly optimal iteration-dependent learning rate. We show here that this modular principle is exactly found in the standard conjugate gradient method applied to a linear system (Hestenes & Stiefel, 1952; Polyak, 1969; Shewchuk, 1994), and therefore the Conjugate Gradient Method represents an automatic stochastic gradient method (AutoSGM), where the algorithm’s hyperparameters are all iterative. Formally, at each iteration \(t\), the parameter vector \(\mathbf{w}[t]\) is updated via the gradient \(\mathbf{g}[t]\) passed through a first-order lowpass filter . \[\boxed{ \begin{aligned} \statez{\mathbf{q}[t]} = \filter{\beta}\,\cdot \statez{\mathbf{q}[t-1]} + \input{\mathbf{g}[t]}\\ \input{\mathbf{v}[t]} = \statez{\mathbf{q}[t]} - \filter{\gamma}\,\cdot \statez{\mathbf{q}[t-1]} \\ \end{aligned} }\] \[\boxed{ \begin{aligned} \state{\mathbf{w}[t+1]} = \state{\mathbf{w}[t]} - \gain{\alpha[t]} \cdot \input{\mathbf{v}[t]}\\ \state{\mathbf{\varepsilon}[t+1]} = \state{\mathbf{\varepsilon}[t]} - \gain{\alpha[t]} \cdot \input{\mathbf{v}[t]} \end{aligned} }\] We will now assume that the filter parameters are possibly iteration-dependent. \[\boxed{ \begin{aligned} \statez{\mathbf{q}[t]} = \filter{\beta[t-1]}\,\cdot \statez{\mathbf{q}[t-1]} + \input{\mathbf{g}[t]}\\ \input{\mathbf{v}[t]} = \statez{\mathbf{q}[t]} - \filter{\gamma[t-1]}\,\cdot \statez{\mathbf{q}[t-1]} \\ \state{\mathbf{w}[t+1]} = \state{\mathbf{w}[t]} - \gain{\alpha[t]} \cdot \input{\mathbf{v}[t]}\\ \end{aligned} }\] . The learning algorithm is designed to minimize the objective . \[f\big(\state{\mathbf{w}[t+1]}\big) = f\big(\state{\mathbf{w}[t]}\big) + \state{\mathbf{\varepsilon}[t+1]}^\intercal \mathbf{R}\,\state{\mathbf{\varepsilon}[t+1]} + \mathbf{n}[t+1]^\intercal \state{\mathbf{\varepsilon}[t+1]}\] where \(\mathbf{R}\) is an \(n\times n\) symmetric positive definite (SPD) matrix, and \(\mathbf{n}[t+1]\) is the output of linear system . \[\boxed{ \mathbf{n}[t+1] = \mathbf{n}[t] + \boldsymbol{\xi}[t] }\] driven by a zero-mean noise process with finite moments, where \(\mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol{\xi}[t]] = 0, \mathbb{E}\|\boldsymbol{\xi}[t]\|^2=\sigma^2< \infty, \mathbb{E}\|\boldsymbol{\xi}[t]\|^4 < \infty.\) . Let \(\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{R} \state{\mathbf{w}^\ast}\), then . \[\boxed{ \begin{aligned} \input{\mathbf{g}[t]} = \mathbf{R} \state{\mathbf{\varepsilon}[t]} + \mathbf{n}[t] = \mathbf{R} \state{\mathbf{w}[t]} - \mathbf{b} + \mathbf{n}[t] \end{aligned}. }\] Therefore, by linearity, we have the gradient generating system . \[\boxed{ \begin{aligned} \input{\mathbf{g}[t+1]} = \mathbf{R} \state{\mathbf{\varepsilon}[t+1]} = \input{\mathbf{g}[t]} - \gain{\alpha[t]}\cdot\mathbf{R}\input{\mathbf{v}[t]} + \boldsymbol{\xi}[t] \end{aligned}. }\] The classic method assumes \(\sigma^2 = 0\), and solves \(\mathbf{R} \state{\mathbf{w}[t]} = \mathbf{b}\). In exact arithmetic, the algorithm converges in at most \(n\) iterations for the linear time-invariant system described by matrix \(\mathbf{R}\). Using the matrix \(\mathbf{R}\)-orthogonality or conjugacy conditions: . \[\boxed{ \begin{aligned} -\input{\mathbf{g}[t+1]}^\intercal \input{\mathbf{v}[t]} = 0.\\ \statez{\mathbf{q}[t+1]}^\intercal \mathbf{R}\statez{\mathbf{q}[t]} = 0. \\ \input{\mathbf{v}[t+1]}^\intercal \mathbf{R}\input{\mathbf{v}[t]} = 0. \end{aligned} }\] where \(\input{\mathbf{g}[t+1]}^\intercal \input{\mathbf{v}[t]} = \state{\mathbf{\varepsilon}[t+1]}^\intercal \mathbf{R}\input{\mathbf{v}[t]}\) . After removing any extraneous scaling constants, for the simplest case \(\sigma^2 = 0\), we will arive at . \[\boxed{ \begin{aligned} \gain{\alpha[t]} = \frac{\input{\mathbf{g}[t]}^\intercal \input{\mathbf{v}[t]}}{\input{\mathbf{v}[t]}^\intercal \mathbf{R}\input{\mathbf{v}[t]}} = \frac{\input{\mathbf{g}[t]}^\intercal \input{\mathbf{g}[t]}}{\input{\mathbf{v}[t]}^\intercal \mathbf{R}\input{\mathbf{v}[t]}} \end{aligned}, }\] \[\boxed{ \begin{aligned} \filter{\beta[t]} = \frac{-\input{\mathbf{g}[t+1]}^\intercal \mathbf{R}\statez{\mathbf{q}[t]}}{\statez{\mathbf{q}[t]}^\intercal \mathbf{R}\statez{\mathbf{q}[t]}} \end{aligned}, }\] \[\boxed{ \begin{aligned} \filter{\gamma[t]} = \filter{\beta[t]} + \frac{\input{\mathbf{g}[t+1]}^\intercal \mathbf{R}\input{\mathbf{v}[t]}}{\statez{\mathbf{q}[t]}^\intercal \mathbf{R}\input{\mathbf{v}[t]}} = \filter{\beta[t]} - \frac{\input{\mathbf{g}[t+1]}^\intercal \input{\mathbf{g}[t+1]}}{\input{\mathbf{g}[t]}^\intercal \input{\mathbf{g}[t]}} \end{aligned}. }\] . | The learning rate satisfies the stationarity condition of the objective function, which is an \(\mathbf{R}\)-orthogonality condition between the next parameter error and the current filter’s output. | The two filter parameters are designed to enforce \(\mathbf{R}\)-orthogonality conditions on successive states and outputs of the first-order filter. Since the update directions \(\mathbf{v}[t]\) are constructed from the filter states \(\mathbf{q}[t]\), imposing these two filter design conditions ensures that successive filter outputs represent non-overlapping information in the \(\mathbf{R}\)-energy space. First, \(\mathbf{q}[t+1]^\intercal\mathbf{R}\mathbf{q}[t]=0\) ensures that the internal filter state does not accumulate redundant components. Second, \(\mathbf{v}[t+1]^\intercal \mathbf{R}\mathbf{v}[t]=0\) enforces conjugacy of the update directions, ensuring that successive steps do not reintroduce previously eliminated components of the error. Any nonzero \(\mathbf{R}\)-inner product between successive filter states and update directions implies residual overlap in the quadratic energy, leading to redundant updates. | . More generally, . \[\boxed{ \begin{aligned} \gain{\alpha[t]} = \frac{\big(\input{\mathbf{g}[t]} + \boldsymbol{\xi}[t]\big)^\intercal \input{\mathbf{v}[t]}}{\input{\mathbf{v}[t]}^\intercal \mathbf{R}\input{\mathbf{v}[t]}} \end{aligned}, }\] \[\boxed{ \begin{aligned} \filter{\beta[t]} = \frac{-\input{\mathbf{g}[t+1]}^\intercal \mathbf{R}\statez{\mathbf{q}[t]}}{\statez{\mathbf{q}[t]}^\intercal \mathbf{R}\statez{\mathbf{q}[t]}} \end{aligned}, }\] \[\boxed{ \begin{aligned} \filter{\gamma[t]} = \filter{\beta[t]} - \frac{\input{\mathbf{g}[t+1]}^\intercal \big(\input{\mathbf{g}[t+1]} - \boldsymbol{\xi}[t]\big)}{\input{\mathbf{g}[t]}^\intercal \big(\input{\mathbf{g}[t]} + \boldsymbol{\xi}[t]\big)} \end{aligned}. }\] . For the simplest case, \(\sigma^2=0\), a possible pseudocode for this particular AutoSGM is: . \[\boxed{ \begin{aligned} \input{\hbox{INITIALIZE}}\\ \epsilon \approx 0\\ \state{\mathbf{w}[0]} = \mathbf{0}\\ \input{\mathbf{g}[0]} = \mathbf{R} \state{\mathbf{w}[0]} - \mathbf{b} + \mathbf{n}[0]\\ \statez{\mathbf{q}[0]} = \mathbf{0} + \input{\mathbf{g}[0]}\\ \input{\mathbf{v}[0]} = \statez{\mathbf{q}[0]} - \mathbf{0} \\ t = 0\\ \input{\hbox{LOOP}}\\ \gain{\alpha[t]} = \frac{\input{\mathbf{g}[t]}^\intercal \input{\mathbf{v}[t]}}{\input{\mathbf{v}[t]}^\intercal \mathbf{R}\input{\mathbf{v}[t]}}\\ \state{\mathbf{w}[t+1]} = \state{\mathbf{w}[t]} - \gain{\alpha[t]} \cdot \input{\mathbf{v}[t]}\\ \\ \input{\hbox{Generate Gradient}}\\ \input{\mathbf{g}[t+1]} = \input{\mathbf{g}[t]} - \gain{\alpha[t]}\cdot\mathbf{R}\input{\mathbf{v}[t]}\\ \input{\hbox{or}}\\ \input{\mathbf{g}[t+1]} = \mathbf{R} \state{\mathbf{w}[t+1]} - \mathbf{b}\\ \input{\hbox{STOP, if }} \input{\mathbf{g}[t+1]}^\intercal \input{\mathbf{g}[t+1]} < \epsilon^2 \\ \\ \filter{\beta[t]} = \frac{-\input{\mathbf{g}[t+1]}^\intercal \mathbf{R}\statez{\mathbf{q}[t]}}{\statez{\mathbf{q}[t]}^\intercal \mathbf{R}\statez{\mathbf{q}[t]}}\\ \filter{\gamma[t]} = \filter{\beta[t]} - \frac{\input{\mathbf{g}[t+1]}^\intercal \input{\mathbf{g}[t+1]}}{\input{\mathbf{g}[t]}^\intercal \input{\mathbf{g}[t]}}\\ \\ \statez{\mathbf{q}[t+1]} = \filter{\beta[t]}\,\cdot \statez{\mathbf{q}[t]} + \input{\mathbf{g}[t+1]}\\ \input{\mathbf{v}[t+1]} = \statez{\mathbf{q}[t+1]} - \filter{\gamma[t]}\,\cdot \statez{\mathbf{q}[t]} \\ t = t+1\\ \input{\hbox{END LOOP}} \end{aligned} }\] . | Hestenes, M. R., & Stiefel, E. (1952). Methods of Conjugate Gradients for Solving Linear Systems. Journal of Research of the National Bureau of Standards, 49(6), 409. https://doi.org/10.6028/jres.049.044 | Polyak, B. T. (1969). The Conjugate Gradient Method in Extremal Problems. USSR Computational Mathematics and Mathematical Physics, 9(4), 94–112. https://doi.org/10.1016/0041-5553(69)90035-4 | Shewchuk, J. R. (1994). An Introduction to the Conjugate Gradient Method without the Agonizing Pain. School of Computer Science, Carnegie Mellon University. | .

16.title: Trust-Region Optimal Learning Rate Principle
16.content: A Modular Principle . Page created: Sep 11 2025 at 12:00 AM . Please cite this page if you use information from these notes for your work, research, or anything that requires academic or formal citation. Oluwasegun Somefun. “Trust-Region Optimal Learning Rate Principle.” 2025. Recall that momentum (Polyak, 1969) is a lowpass regularized stochastic gradient algorithm. One that applies a first-order lowpass filter to its stochastic gradient, before multiplying by a possibly optimal iteration-dependent learning rate. Here, more specifically, we show how Adaptive Moment Estimation (Adam) and Momentum Orthogonalized with Newton-Schultz (Muon) encode the principle of trust-region optimal learning rates for the lowpass regularized stochastic gradient algorithm.

17.title: Analytical Overview: The Trust-Region Principle in Automated Learning-Rate Selection

18.title: 1. The Gap Between Heuristics and First Principles
18.content: In the current state of deep learning optimization, the transition from initial weights to a trained model is often navigated through a collection of trial-and-error heuristics. Popular mechanisms like momentum and learning-rate annealing are frequently treated as “black box” tools—accelerators and cooling schedules—rather than components of a rigorous mathematical system. This lack of first-principles clarity creates a significant bottleneck in our ability to automate and optimize the learning process. The Signal Processing Viewpoint bridges this gap by interpreting optimization as a feedback control task. Under this lens, momentum is formalized as a lowpass filtering operation on noisy gradients, while annealing is recognized as the application of window functions to a vanishing trust-region. This shift from heuristics to \(H_{\beta,\gamma}(z)\) transfer functions provides the mathematical rigor required to move optimization into a structured signal processing framework. | Current Heuristic View | Proposed Signal Processing Interpretation | . | Momentum: An “accelerator” used to push past flat spots or oscillate less in narrow valleys. | Lowpass Filtering: A first-order recursive filter (\(H_{\beta,\gamma}(z)\)) that extracts the underlying signal from noisy stochastic gradients. | . | Annealing: A cooling schedule (e.g., cosine) to reduce the step size as the model approaches a minimum. | Windowing: A smoothness-constrained shaping function that governs the contraction of the trust-region radius over a finite window. | . | Second-order Normalizers: “Smart” optimizers (Adam/RMSProp) that adapt rates per parameter based on history. | Optimal Filtering: A data-driven realization of a trust-region subproblem, where steps are normalized by the RMS of the gradient. | . Moving from these interpretations to formal mathematics requires a simplified environment to build the necessary intuition regarding stability and convergence.

19.title: 2. Conceptual Foundation: The 1-D Parameter Model
19.content: By analyzing a single-parameter quadratic model, we can expose the underlying mechanics of stochastic gradient updates without the noise of tensor algebra. This environment reveals that convergence is a matter of certifying the stability of a linear iteration-invariant system. Three Essential Takeaways from 1-D Analysis . | Stability Boundaries and the Data Scale Convergence is fundamentally constrained by the data’s second moment (variance), \(\lambda\). For a basic stochastic gradient update to remain stable, the learning rate \(\alpha\) must stay within a strict boundary. | The “Fastest Mode” and Momentum Dynamics In a plain system, the fastest convergence occurs when \(\alpha = \frac{1}{\lambda}.\) However, when gradient smoothing (momentum) is introduced, the fastest mode shifts to a damped complex-conjugate root case. Here, for a given pole \(\beta\), the optimal zero is \(\gamma = \frac{\beta}{1+\beta},\) and the optimal learning rate is \(\alpha = \frac{1+\beta}{\eta \lambda}.\) . | Conditioning via Lowpass Regularization Introducing momentum changes the system matrix from a rank-one system (where the expected error and error change are coupled) to a full-rank system. This “Lowpass Regularization” improves the conditioning of the expected error dynamics, expanding the space of stable learning rates and smoothing the effective loss surface. | . Critical Stability Condition For a simple stochastic gradient update to remain stable and avoid divergence, the learning rate \(\alpha\) must satisfy the following condition relative to the data variance \(\lambda\): \(0 < \alpha < \frac{2}{\lambda}.\) . However, the fixed scale of the 1-D model fails in the non-stationary landscapes of deep learning, necessitating a more robust mathematical constraint: the Trust-Region.

20.title: 3. Demystifying the Trust-Region Subproblem
20.content: The Trust-Region Principle assumes that our local gradient information is only reliable within a small neighborhood of the current parameters. In automated learning-rate selection, we solve a Trust-Region Parameter Error Minimization Subproblem at every iteration. We aim to minimize the expected squared error \(\mathbb{E}[\epsilon^2(t+1, i)]\) subject to a constraint on the step-size: \(\mathbb{E}[\Delta^2(t+1, i)] \le \rho^2.\) . Solving this constrained optimization leads directly to an optimal iteration-dependent learning rate \(\alpha(t, i)\). This derived rate is not a fixed hyperparameter but a ratio that balances the alignment of parameters and gradients against the noise of the environment. The solution to the trust-region subproblem identifies the optimal learning rate as: \(\alpha(t, i) = \mu \frac{a(t,i)}{d(t,i)}.\) . | The Numerator \(a(t, i)\): A correlation-based term estimating the partial-correlation between the parameter and the normalized gradient. | The Denominator \(d(t, i)\): The root of the second moment (RMS) of the gradient. | The Principle: This ratio ensures that steps are inversely proportional to the gradient scale, providing the “missing link” that justifies second-order gradient normalizers like Adam. | . While \(a(t, i)\) is often approximated as 1 in practical realizations, maintaining the iteration-dependent numerator leads to significant empirical gains in large-scale benchmarks like GPT-2 and Vision Transformers (ViT).

21.title: 4. AutoSGM: A Unified Framework for Momentum
21.content: The AutoSGM (Automatic Stochastic Gradient Method) framework formalizes the “Momentum as a Filter” viewpoint. It demonstrates that the most popular optimizers in the field—Polyak’s Heavy Ball (PHB), Nesterov’s Accelerated Gradient (NAG), and Adam—are not distinct species of algorithms. Instead, they are specific configurations of a first-order lowpass filter \(H_{\beta,\gamma}(z)\) acting as a controller in a feedback loop with the gradient-generating oracle. | Algorithm | Zero Location (\(\gamma\)) | Learning Rate Type | Mathematical Interpretation | . | Polyak’s Heavy Ball (PHB) | Fixed at 0 | Iteration-Dependent | Standard lowpass smoothing; rank-one to full-rank transition | . | Nesterov (NAG) | \(\beta/(1+\beta)\) | Iteration-Dependent | Optimal pole-zero placement; collapses complex eigenvalues to zero | . | Adam | Fixed at 0 | Fixed Numerator (\(a=1\)) | Approximate realization of the trust-region subproblem | . Empirical evaluations on GPT-2 and ViT demonstrate that the iteration-dependent numerator (Equation 2.9) consistently outperforms the fixed-numerator realization (Adam), proving that the full Trust-Region theory provides a more effective path for accelerated learning than the simplified heuristics used in current practice.

22.title: 5. Annealing as Optimal Windowing
22.content: “Annealing” is the process of gradually contracting the trust-region radius \(\delta(t)\) over the duration of training. In the Signal Processing Viewpoint, these schedules are identified as Vanishing Trust-Regions. They are not arbitrary; they are the unique solutions to Dirichlet Energy Minimization problems—variational functionals that seek the smoothest possible transition between fixed endpoints. The Three Optimal Window Functions . | Linear Decay: The unique minimizer of the Dirichlet energy of \(\delta(t)\). It provides the minimal per-iteration variation of the radius. | Cosine Annealing: The solution to a Regularized Dirichlet Energy Minimization problem. By minimizing variation while constraining the average magnitude, it produces a Lipschitz-smooth taper with a slower initial decay, allowing the model more time for exploration. | Square-root Linear Decay: The minimizer of the energy of the squared radius \(\delta^2(t)\). This schedule remains larger for longer, providing an aggressive contraction only at the very boundary of the training window. | . The Vanishing Window Ratio Logic Stability is governed by the ratio \(\frac{\delta(t)}{\delta(t-1)}.\) In these optimal windows, the radius \(\delta(t)\) vanishes strictly faster than the ratio of successive radii, effectively dampening the contribution of the stochastic gradient as the model nears convergence.

23.title: 6. Summary of Key Insights for the Aspiring Learner
23.content: The Trust-Region principle transforms optimization from a black box of heuristics into a structured signal processing task. | Lowpass Regularization is Fundamental: Smoothing the gradient is approximately equivalent to smoothing the actual loss surface. | The Unified Selection Principle: PHB, NAG, and Adam exist on a single spectrum of pole-zero placement defined by \(H_{\beta,\gamma}(z)\). | Steps Must Be Scale-Inverse: Effective updates are inversely proportional to the gradient scale \(1/d(t,i)\). | Smoothness via Dirichlet Functionals: Learning-rate schedules should be derived from variational principles using Lipschitz-smooth tapers. | . By treating optimization as a challenge of signal estimation and control, we transform the training process into a predictable, first-principles task. | Polyak, B. T. (1969). The Conjugate Gradient Method in Extremal Problems. USSR Computational Mathematics and Mathematical Physics, 9(4), 94–112. https://doi.org/10.1016/0041-5553(69)90035-4 | .

24.title: Momentum as Lowpass Filter Design
24.content: Fastest mean convergence can be achieved without Momentum. Sep 11 2025 . Please cite this page if you use information from these notes for your work, research, or anything that requires academic or formal citation. Oluwasegun Somefun. “Momentum as Lowpass Filter Design.” 2025. | Momentum as Lowpass Filter Design . | Understanding Momentum as Lowpass Regularization | Setup: A Minimalist Stochastic Error Model . | Baseline: Plain Stochastic Gradient Method | . | Expected Error Dynamics . | Structural Insight | . | The First-order Lowpass Filter . | Transfer Function | Lowpass Configuration | Direct Realization | Direct Canonical Realization | Regularized Form | Non-Expansive Power Gain Property | . | Full-Rank Regularization of the Expected Error Dynamics . | Stability Certificate | Fastest Convergence | Result: Nesterov Momentum (NAG) . | Stability Region | . | Compare: Heavy-Ball Momentum (HB) . | Stability Region | . | . | Takeaways . | Acceleration Isn’t Always the Point | Open Question | Reality Check | . | . | . Here we introduce the concept of momentum (Polyak, 1969) as a lowpass regularized stochastic gradient algorithm. One that applies a first-order lowpass filter to its stochastic gradient, before multiplying by a possibly optimal iteration-dependent learning rate. For analytic purpose, we will study a minimal stochastic system, for which the algorithm’s hyperparameters are assumed time-invariant. More specifically, we show how Nesterov’s momentum arise a solution to the fastest mean error convergence of this problem. More importantly, we show that structurally, the Plain stochastic gradient method, Heavy-Ball momentum, and Nesterov’s momentum correspond fundamentally to different different filter designs of the same algorithm.

25.title: Understanding Momentum as Lowpass Regularization
25.content: Traditionally, the way we talk about training neural networks is often rooted in classical mechanics. We describe momentum as a ball rolling down a hilly landscape, gaining inertia to carry it over local bumps. Alternatively, another popular interpretation is that momentum estimates the first moment of a gradient. While these physical analogies are often intuitive, they obscure the underlying discrete, iteration-level mechanics of the learning algorithm. Our central claim is that a literal mathematical interpretation already exists via classical signal processing tools related to first-order lowpass filters. Momentum is mathematically equivalent to passing stochastic gradients through a first-order linear lowpass filter. The filter, denoted \(\filter{\sH_{\beta,\gamma}}\) is configured via two variables, its pole \(\filter{\beta}\) and zero \(\filter{\gamma}\). By shifting the pole and zero locations of the filter, we can create different flavors of momentum. We will soon see that this filter design interpretation provides a principled dynamical regularization perspective into the behavior of classic momentum variants, and can improve the way we design stochastic gradient learning algorithms for training AI models.

26.title: Setup: A Minimalist Stochastic Error Model
26.content: To understand what fundamentally drives learning dynamics, it helps to strip the system down to its bare essentials. To make our position concrete and analytically transparent, we deliberately adopt a minimalist setting. By stripping away the tensor algebra of high-dimensional neural networks, we expose the error dynamics and underlying structure of stochastic gradient learning in its simplest form. Consider a single parameter model defined by the twice-differentiable weighted quadratic function . \[f(\rw[t]) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\psi^2[t]\,\varepsilon^2[t],\] where . | \(\rw[t] \in \sR^{1}\) (Parameter) denotes the model parameter at a current iteration \(t\). | \(\varepsilon[t]\) (Error) defined as \(\varepsilon[t]=\rw[t] - \rw^\star\) represents distance of the current parameter value from its optimum value, \(\rw^\star\). The current error change due to a parameter update is defined as \(\Delta[t+1]=\varepsilon[t+1]- \varepsilon[t]\). | \(\psi[t] \in \sR^{1}\) (Data Process) is a realization of a zero-mean, wide-sense stationary stochastic data process for which its kurtosis, \(\kappa\ge1\) exists. We also assume that \(\psi[t]\) is statistically independent of both \(\varepsilon[t]\) and \(\psi[l]\), $l \neq t$. | \(\lambda\) (Scale) denotes the second moment of the stochastic process, defined by \(\mathbb{E}[\psi^2[t]]=\lambda\), and \(\mathbb{E}[\psi^4[t]]=\kappa\,\lambda^2\), \(\lambda>0\). | . Differentiating \(f(\rw[t])\) with respect to \(\rw[t]\) reveals a key structural insight. The gradient and Hessian take the form . \[\rg[t] = \psi^2[t]\,\varepsilon[t], \qquad \rmH[t] = \psi^2[t].\] In expectation, . \[\E[\rg[t]] = \lambda\,\E[\varepsilon[t]], \qquad \E[\rmH[t]] = \lambda.\] Our primary objective in this analysis, is to drive the expected error \(\E[\varepsilon[t]]\) to converge the fastest to its optimum value of zero, starting from any initial non-zero value. Now that the environment is set, we start with the classic algorithm, the plain stochastic gradient method. Baseline: Plain Stochastic Gradient Method . At iteration $t$, this update rule . \[\varepsilon[t+1] = \varepsilon[t] - \gain{\alpha[t]}\,\rg[t]\] receives a learning-rate estimate $\gain{\alpha[t]}$ from a learning-rate oracle and a gradient estimate $\rg[t]$ from a gradient-generating oracle. We will further assume that $\gain{\alpha[t]}=\gain{\alpha} > 0, \forall\, t$. Therefore . \[\varepsilon[t+1] = \varepsilon[t] - \gain{\alpha}\,\rg[t].\] Next, we will see that ensuring mean convergence in this problem setup is equivalent to certifying the stability conditions of a linear iteration-invariant system.

27.title: Expected Error Dynamics
27.content: The expected error and expected error change for the plain SGM, forms a two-state dynamical system . \[\underbrace{ \begin{bmatrix} \E[\varepsilon[t]]\\ \E[\Delta[t+1]] \end{bmatrix}}_{\rvx[t]} = \underbrace{ \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ k_p & a_p \end{bmatrix} }_{\rmA} \, \underbrace{ \begin{bmatrix} \E[\varepsilon[t-1]]\\ \E[\Delta[t]] \end{bmatrix}}_{\rvx[t-1]}\] where \(a_p = -\gain{\alpha}\,\lambda\), and \(k_p = -\gain{\alpha}\,\lambda\). Reasons for separately naming the coefficients will soon become clear. The system can also be compactly written as . \[\rvx[t] = \rmA^t\,\rvx[0],\quad t \ge 0,\] and is a rank-1 system because \(a_p = k_p\,\). The exponential stability and asymptotic decay to zero of the mean error variable and mean error change variable requires that the two eigenvalues of \(\rmA\) given by $z_1 = 0$, and \({z_2 =1-\gain{\alpha}\,\lambda}\) (which can be obtained via \(\det(z\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}) = z(z-(1-\gain{\alpha}\,\lambda)) = 0\)) to have a magnitude strictly less than 1 (Rugh, 1996). This stability condition is satisfied if and only if . \[0 < \gain{\alpha}\,\lambda < 2.\] By taking any standard norm $\|\cdot\|$ (1-norm, 2-norm, $\infty$-norm) of $\rx[t]$, with the corresponding induced matrix norm, and then exploiting the rank-1 non-symmetric structure of \(\rmA\), a certificate on the exponential convergence of the expected error states, for \(t\ge 0\), can be obtained as . \[\begin{align} {\| \rvx[t+1] \| \le c \cdot |{z_2}|^{t}\, \|\rvx[0]\|}, \end{align}\] where $c\ge 1$ is a positive constant determined by the left and right eigenvectors corresponding to the dominant (nonzero) eigenvalue \(z_2\), as well as on the chosen induced matrix norm of \(\rmA\). Structural Insight . The fastest possible mode of convergence, $z_2 = 0$, is achieved by choosing . \[\gain{\alpha} = \tfrac{1}{\lambda}.\] This yields deadbeat behavior (the expected error converges in a single iteration). However, deadbeat behavior of the expected error dynamics is generally undesirable in practice, and not ideal due to its high sensitivity to variability of the stochastic gradient. Instead, directly minimizing the mean-squared error at the cost of a slower mean error contraction per iteration would result in . \[\gain{\alpha} = \mu\,\tfrac{1}{\lambda}, \qquad \mu = \tfrac{1}{3}.\] The fundamental limitation here is structural. The mean error dynamics is inherently rank-1 for any choice of learning rate. To remove this limitation, we now regularize the gradient using a lowpass filter. By treating the gradient as a signal, we can transition to a much general algorithmic framework. In signal processing, a lowpass filter attenuates high-frequency components of its input signal while preserving its low-frequency behavior. We will refer to this spectral shaping of the gradient as lowpass regularization.

28.title: The First-order Lowpass Filter
28.content: Now, instead of using gradient \(\rg[t]\), we replace it with a filtered version $\rv[t] = \filter{\sH_{\beta,\gamma}}\{\rg[t]\}$. Transfer Function . The behavior of the filtered gradient is governed by the transfer-function . \[\filter{\sH_{\beta,\gamma}}(z) = \eta \frac{1-\filter{\gamma}\,z^{-1}}{1-\filter{\beta}\,z^{-1}}.\] where \(z^{-1}\) denotes a one-step delay operator. Lowpass Configuration . The filter has a single pole location $\beta$, zero location $\gamma$, and positive gain constant $\eta$ satisfying . \[\absv{\filter{\beta}} < 1, \quad \filter{\gamma} < \filter{\beta}, \quad \eta = \tfrac{1-\filter{\beta}}{1-\filter{\gamma}}\] ensuring bounded-input, bounded-output (BIBO) stability, and a unit steady-state (or zero-frequency) gain. Direct Realization . A direct difference equation realization of the first-order filter with input \(\rg[t]\) is . \[\begin{align*} \begin{aligned} \rv[t] &= \filter{\beta} \, \rv[t-1] + \eta\,\big(\rg[t] - \filter{\gamma} \, \rg[t-1] \big). \end{aligned} \end{align*}\] The update rule in terms of the parameter error is now . \[\begin{align*} \begin{aligned} \varepsilon[t+1] &= \varepsilon[t] - \gain{\alpha}\,\rv[t]. \end{aligned} \end{align*}\] However, note that this is an inefficient realization, since there are two states kept. Direct Canonical Realization . For any choice of \((\filter{\beta}, \filter{\gamma})\), the direct minimal state canonical realization of the filter, where \(\rq[t]\) is the filter’s state is . \[\begin{align*} \begin{aligned} \rq[t] &= \filter{\beta}\,\rq[t-1] + \rg[t]\\ \rv[t] &= \filter{\eta}\bigl(\rq[t] - \filter{\gamma}\,q[t-1]\bigr)\\ \varepsilon[t+1] &= \varepsilon[t] - \gain{\alpha}\,\rv[t]. \end{aligned} \end{align*}\] Note that $\eta$ can also be absorbed into the learning-rate. Regularized Form . Compactly, for any appropriate initialization of \(\rq[-1]\), and denoting \(\delta=\filter{\beta}-\filter{\gamma}\), we have . \[\begin{align*} \begin{aligned} \varepsilon[t+1] &= \varepsilon[t] - \gain{\alpha}\,\filter{\eta}\,\Bigl(\rg[t] + \delta\,\filter{\beta}^{t}\rq[-1] + \delta\sum_{i=0}^{t-1}\filter{\beta}^{i}\,\rg[t-1-i]\Bigr),\quad t \ge 0. \end{aligned} \end{align*}\] Non-Expansive Power Gain Property . Mathematically, this lowpass filter’s energy is non-expansive because its power gain . \[|\filter{\sH_{\beta,\gamma}}(e^{j\,\omega})|^2 \le 1,\] for all frequencies \(\omega=[-\pi, \pi]\). This ensures that the filter’s output never contains more energy or variance than its input. Therefore . \[\E[\rv^2[t]] \le \E[\rg^2[t]].\] The gradient filter spectrally regulates how gradient information is attenuated or amplified over iterations. With the filter smoothing the gradient input, we must now peek into how it reshapes the mean-error dynamics.

29.title: Full-Rank Regularization of the Expected Error Dynamics
29.content: With the lowpass filter, the system matrix \(\rmA\) still retains its two-state form, \(\rmA = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ k_p & a_p \end{bmatrix}.\) . However, the constants \(k_p\neq a_p\), indicating that the introduction of gradient filtering transforms \(\rmA\) into a full-rank system, where . \[a_p = \filter{\beta}-\gain{\alpha}\eta\lambda, \quad k_p = -\gain{\alpha}\eta\lambda(1-\filter{\gamma}).\] The characteristic polynomial is \(z^2 - (1+a_p)z + (a_p - k_p) = 0.\) The stability of this system is determined by the eigenvalues or roots \((z_1, z_2)\) of \(\rmA\). Since this is a quadratic equation, the discriminant: \(D = (1 + a_p)^2 - 4(a_p - k_p)\) can either be: . | $D = 0$: (two real, repeated roots), | $D > 0$: (two real, distinct roots), or | $D < 0$: (two complex, conjugate roots). | . Stability Certificate . For stability, the eigenvalues must remain within the unit circle: \(|z_1| \le |z_2| < 1.\) . Fastest Convergence . The fastest mode of convergence (\(z_1 = z_2 = 0\)) of the expected error dynamics occurs if and only if both \(1+a_p =0\) and \(a_p-k_p=0.\) This leads to the following configuration. Choose the of the zero location of the filter as . \[\filter{\gamma} = \frac{\filter{\beta}}{1+\filter{\beta}}\] and the learning rate such that: . \[\gain{\alpha} \eta \lambda = 1 + \filter{\beta}\] for any choice of the filter pole $\absv{\filter{\beta}} < 1$. To see the realization of this mean-error optimal filter zero configuration in action . Result: Nesterov Momentum (NAG) . Plugging \(\filter{\gamma}=\frac{\filter{\beta}}{1+\filter{\beta}}\) into the canonical filter realization yields . \[\begin{aligned} &\rq[t] = \filter{\beta} \, \rq[t-1] + (1-\filter{\beta})\,\rg[t]\\ &\rv[t] = \filter{\beta}\,\rq[t] + (1-\filter{\beta})\,\rg[t]\\ &\varepsilon[t+1] = \varepsilon[t] - \gain{\alpha} \, \rv[t]. \end{aligned}\] Equivalently, the \(1-\filter{\beta}\) factor can be absorbed into the learning-rate yielding \(\bar{\gain{\alpha}}=\gain{\alpha}(1-\filter{\beta})\) for any choice of $\gain{\alpha}$. The canonical realization then becomes . \[\begin{aligned} &\rq[t] = \filter{\beta} \, \rq[t-1] + \rg[t]\\ &\rv[t] = \filter{\beta}\,\rq[t] + \rg[t]\\ &\varepsilon[t+1] = \varepsilon[t] - \bar{\gain{\alpha}} \, \rv[t], \end{aligned}\] These realizations of the first-order lowpass filter \(\sH_{\filter{\beta},\filter{\gamma}}\) acting on \(\rg[t]\) have been called Nesterov Accelerated Gradient (NAG). Therefore, the popular Nesterov momentum realization of the parameter update is exactly recovered when \(\filter{\gamma}=\frac{\filter{\beta}}{1+\filter{\beta}}\). Using the deadbeat learning-rate, \(\bar{\gain{\alpha}}\) simplifies to \(\bar{\gain{\alpha}} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\). Stability Region . We can analyze the characteristic polynomial using Jury stability conditions, to get that for \(0 \le \filter{\beta} < 1\): . \[0 < \gain{\alpha} \eta \lambda < \frac{2(1+\filter{\beta})^2}{1+2\filter{\beta}}\] We now compare a trivial configuration of the zero location of the filter, $\filter{\gamma} = 0$. Compare: Heavy-Ball Momentum (HB) . Now consider the filter design corresponding to $\filter{\gamma}=0$ and $0\le\filter{\beta}<1$. This leads to $a_p-k_p=\filter{\beta}$, and the characteristic polynomial $z^2 -(1+a_p)\,z + (a_p-k_p) = 0 $ simplifies to $z^2 - (1+a_p)\,z + \filter{\beta} = 0 $ with discriminant $D = (1+a_p)^2 - 4\,\filter{\beta}$. Setting $1+a_p =1+\filter{\beta}-\gain{\alpha}\eta\lambda=0$ gives $\gain{\alpha}\eta\lambda={1+\filter{\beta}}$, the characteristic polynomial becomes $z^2 + \filter{\beta} = 0 $ and the discriminant is minimized as $D =-4\filter{\beta}$. With this choice, the two system eigenvalues of \(\rmA\) satisfy a common magnitude \(\lvert z_1 \rvert = \lvert z_2 \rvert = \sqrt{\absv{\filter{\beta}}}< 1,\) which ensures exponential stability with decay rate $\sqrt{\absv{\filter{\beta}}}<1$. For the lowpass filter case $0<\filter{\beta}<1$, the eigenvalues form a complex‑conjugate pair $z_{1,2}=\pm\,j\sqrt{\filter{\beta}}$, corresponding to $D=-4\filter{\beta}<0$. For the no filtering case $\filter{\beta}=0$, the eigenvalues degenerate to repeated values $\lvert z_1 \rvert = \lvert z_2 \rvert= 0$ yielding the fastest possible expected error decay and so $D=-4\filter{\beta}=0$. Generally, the characteristic polynomial $z^2 - (1+a_p)\,z + \beta=0$ satisfies $z_1+z_2 = 1+a_p$ and $z_1\,z_2=\filter{\beta}$. Solving the discriminant $D\le 0$ for $\gain{\alpha}$ leads to the learning-rate interval . \[\tfrac{(1-\sqrt{\absv{\filter{\beta}}})^2}{\eta}\, \tfrac{1}{\lambda} \le \gain{\alpha} \le \tfrac{(1+\sqrt{\absv{\filter{\beta}}})^2}{\eta}\, \tfrac{1}{\lambda}.\] The midpoint of this interval $\gain{\alpha} = \frac{1+\filter{\beta}}{\eta}\, \frac{1}{\lambda}= \frac{1+\filter{\beta}}{1-\filter{\beta}}\,\frac{1}{\lambda}$ corresponds to $1+a_p=0$. Consequently, as $\filter{\beta} \to 0$, and $\gain{\alpha} \to \frac{1}{\lambda}$ then both the plain SGM update form and the fastest possible mean convergence mode is recovered in the limit. In summary, the filter design choice $\filter{\gamma}=0$ and $0\le\filter{\beta}<1$ induces a mean error dynamics whose exponential stability or decay rate depends solely on the pole location ${\filter{\beta}}$. To implement this filter design of the parameter update, the canonical realization yields . \[\begin{aligned} &\rv[t] = \filter{\beta}\,\rv[t-1] + (1-\filter{\beta})\,\rg[t]\\ &\varepsilon[t+1] = \varepsilon[t] - \gain{\alpha} \, \rv[t], \end{aligned}\] which recovers the Heavy-Ball (HB) momentum form of the parameter update. Equivalently . \[\begin{aligned} &\rv[t] = \filter{\beta}\,\rv[t-1] + \rg[t]\\ &\varepsilon[t+1] = \varepsilon[t] - \bar{\gain{\alpha}} \, \rv[t], \end{aligned}\] where $\bar{\gain{\alpha}}=\gain{\alpha}(1-\filter{\beta})$. Recall that here, the first moment of the gradient is $\E[\rg[t]] = \lambda\,\E[\varepsilon[t]]$. The output of momentum, being the result of a lowpass filter applied to the gradient sequence, is in general neither equal nor pointwise close to the gradient nor to its expectation at any iteration, except in the degenerate case of exact pole-zero cancellation: $\gamma=\beta$, under which $\filter{\sH_{\beta,\gamma}}(z)$ reduces to an effective all-pass filter response. The statistical interpretation of the filter as a first-moment estimator arises only by replacing the exponentially weighted convolution in the iteration-domain with an ensemble expectation. This action explicitly requires an ergodicity assumption so that a weighted time-average along a single realization of the stochastic process can be identified with an ensemble mean. Therefore, in general and especially in non-stationary stochastic gradient regimes, ergodic assumptions are not valid. Consequently, the first moment estimation interpretation breaks down mathematically, while the lowpass filtering characterization of momentum always remains exact and assumption-free. Stability Region . Here, for \(\gamma=0,\, 0 \le \filter{\beta} < 1\): . \[0 < \gain{\alpha} {\eta} \lambda < 2(1+\filter{\beta})\] .

30.title: Takeaways
30.content: The two pillars of momentum, Polyak’s Heavy-Ball (HB) and Nesterov Accelerated Gradient (NAG), are often viewed as distinct algorithms. Through our filter lens, however, they are simply different configurations of the same system. Polyak’s Heavy-Ball (HB) and Nesterov Accelerated Gradient (NAG) are specific choices of the zero location $\filter{\gamma}$ in a lowpass filter, while Plain SGM places both the pole and zero of this filter at the origin ($\filter{\gamma}=\filter{\beta}=0$). Acceleration Isn’t Always the Point . Due to roots in classical mechanics, we often think that the momentum here exists to accelerate optimization. As observed in this setup, using momentum does not lead to a faster mean convergence than the plain stochastic gradient method. The fastest possible mean convergence can be achieved without momentum. So why use momentum? Trivally, because stochastic gradients imply a rough, noisy error surface. Momentum is a lowpass filter, attenuating high-frequency noise. Contrary to popular belief, the principal benefit of momentum is not in acceleration alone, but in dynamical regularization of the parameter update steps. Our position here is that momentum’s primary benefit is dynamic regularization. In general, we see that by regularizing the gradient via the lowpass filter, we get the following properties: . | Noise Attenuation (Spectral Smoothing) preserving the low-frequency structure in the data process while reducing high-frequency noise variance in the gradient | Energy Non-Expansiveness ensuring the update rule never amplifies noise in the gradient. | Expanded Stability Region increases the allowable range of \(\alpha\) before divergence. | . Open Question . If momentum is just a linear first-order filter, what unexplored data efficient, nonlinear filter designs could unlock unseen optimization gains for AI? . Reality Check . These insights are derived under simplifying assumptions. Real deep learning systems are: . | High-dimensional | Non-stationary | Heavy-tailed | . Therefore, note that in real settings, the: . | Structural roles (filtering structure + learning-rate form) generalize | Optimal parameter values do not. | . Conclusion . By understanding momentum as a signal processing filter, we replace the concept of momentum with interpretable pole-zero placements that dynamically regularize gradient information entering our stochastic gradient optimization algorithms. | Polyak, B. T. (1969). The Conjugate Gradient Method in Extremal Problems. USSR Computational Mathematics and Mathematical Physics, 9(4), 94–112. https://doi.org/10.1016/0041-5553(69)90035-4 | Rugh, W. J. (1996). Linear System Theory (2nd ed.). Prentice-Hall. | .

31.title: Learning Rate Design as Trust-Region Normalization
31.content: A Modular View of Modern Optimizers . Sep 11 2025 . Please cite this page if you use information from these notes for your work, research, or anything that requires academic or formal citation. Oluwasegun Somefun. “Learning Rate Design as Trust-Region Normalization.” 2025. | Learning Rate Design as Trust-Region Normalization . | So What?: Modular Design and Trust Regions | Gradient Normalization | Modular Design Principle . | Adam (Vectorized Formulation) . | Scaling Choice | Trust-region Constraint | Realization | Summary | . | Muon (Matrix-valued Operator-level Formulation) . | Scaling Choice | Trust-region Constraint | Implementation | . | Unified Insight | . | Conclusion: Your New Perspective on Learning and Optimizers . | 3-Point Takeaway | Open Question | . | . | . Recall that momentum (Polyak, 1969) is a lowpass regularized stochastic gradient algorithm. One that applies a first-order lowpass filter to its stochastic gradient, before multiplying by a possibly optimal iteration-dependent learning-rate. Here, more specifically, we show how popular lowpass regularized stochastic gradient algorithms, Adaptive Moment Estimation (Adam) and Momentum Orthogonalized with Newton-Schultz (Muon) used to train modern AI models encode trust-region principles in their learning-rate object. Form the setup in Part 1: Momentum as Lowpass Filter Design, recall that the learning-rates derived take the typical form $\gain{\alpha} \propto \lambda^{-1}$. More importantly, recall that the fastest mean-square convergence of the baseline confguration occurs when . \[\gain{\alpha} = \mu\,\tfrac{1}{\lambda}, \qquad \mu = \tfrac{1}{3}.\] This motivates the use of learning-rate rules of the form $\gain{\alpha}=\gain{\mu}\,\lambda^{-1}$ with $0<\gain{\mu}<1$, which trades faster mean convergence for reduced error variance.

32.title: So What?: Modular Design and Trust Regions
32.content: Beyond this specific setup, the rule $\gain{\alpha} \propto \lambda^{-1}$ can be interpreted as expressing a normalization principle that scales parameter update steps inversely with $\lambda$, a characteristic measure (or scale) of the gradient magnitude. Under this interpretation, $\gain{\mu}$ is analogous to a trust-region radius (Conn et al., 2000), and controls the magnitude of the update step. In general, this principle can be realized in practice via iterative data-driven substitutes $\lambda[t]$ for the fixed scale $\lambda$. Here, we assume that $\lambda^{-1}[t]$ is updated during each iteration in some appropriate manner. Interpreting $\lambda$ in this way cleanly separates momentum (gradient smoothing through a lowpass filter) and learning-rate selection (via gradient normalization) into modular components. Therefore, stochastic gradient algorithms for more complicated stochastic setups can be designed to implement a modular strategy consisting of two principles: . | Gradient Filtering via a Lopass Filter: Designing \(\filter{\beta}, \filter{\gamma}\). | Gradient Normalization via the Learning-rate: Scaling by a data-driven substitute for \(1/\lambda\). | .

33.title: Gradient Normalization
33.content: We have seen gradient filtering, the first module of this framework. The second module of the framework is the learning-rate subsystem. We define the general update rule as . \[\rw[t+1] = \rw[t] - \gain{\alpha[t]}\,\rv[t]\] where the learning-rate . \[\gain{\alpha[t]} = \gain{\mu}\,\lambda^{-1}[t]\] effectively normalizes the filtered gradient to satisfy a trust-region constraint, using: . | \(\gain{\mu}\) = fixed trust-region radius | \(\lambda[t]\) = possibly iterative gradient scale | .

34.title: Modular Design Principle
34.content: [1] Gradient Filter Design Choose \(\filter{\beta}, \filter{\gamma}\) to shape the gradient signal. \[\rv[t] = \filter{\sH_{\beta,\gamma}}\{\rg[t]\},\] This may be replaced by any appropriate gradient filter. [2] Gradient Normalization: Apply a trust-region scaling $\gain{\mu}\,\lambda^{-1}[t]$ to normalize the gradient. \[\Delta[t+1] = -\gain{\mu}\,\lambda^{-1}[t]\,\rv[t],\] Normalize by \(\lambda^{-1}[t]\), to get \(\bar{\rv}[t] = \lambda^{-1}[t]\,\rv[t]\) then take a step \(\Delta[t+1] = -\gain{\mu}\,\bar{\rv}[t]\) within a trust-region radius \(\gain{\mu}\). This may be replaced by any appropriate trust-region principle. [3] Parameter Update: Then update the parameter using the trust-region constrained $\Delta[t+1]$. \[\rw[t+1] = \rw[t] + \Delta[t+1].\] To briefly make this concrete, we will now show that this object-oriented modular approach generalizes to other settings beyond this simple setup. Specifically, popular modern deep learning optimizers like Adam and Muon implement these two principles. Adam (Vectorized Formulation) . We now replace the scalar parameter update formulation in this setup with a per-coordinate parameter vector update form. Let $\rw[t],\;\rg[t],\;\rv[t],\;\Delta[t+1]\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times 1}$, where $n \ge 1$, and let the learning rate be $\gain{\alpha[t]}\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times 1}$. Scaling Choice . For each coordinate $i=1,\ldots,n$ of the parameter update step $\Delta[t+1,i] = \gain{\mu}\,\lambda^{-1}[t,i]\,\rv[t,i]$, choose the scale for normalization in the learning-rate vector as . \[\lambda[t,i] = C^{\frac{1}{2}}[t,i],\quad \text{where} \quad C[t] = \E\{\rg^2[t,i]\}.\] which involves estimating the second moment or power of $\rg[t,i]$ for each coordinate. Consequently, since \(\E\{\Delta^{2}[t+1,i]\} = \gain{\alpha}^2[t,i]\,\E\{\rv^{2}[t,i]\}\), and recall that \(\E\{\rv^{2}[t,i]\} \le \E\{\rg^{2}[t,i]\}\), then using $\gain{\alpha}^2[t,i]= \gain{\mu}^2\,\lambda^{-2}[t,i]=\gain{\mu}^2\,C^{-1}[t,i]$ yields . \[\E\{\Delta^{2}[t+1,i]\} \le \gain{\mu}^2,\] with equality if $\gamma=\beta=0$. Trust-region Constraint . This scaling choice can be interpreted as ensuring that under the parameter update step $\Delta[t+1] = \gain{\mu}\,\lambda^{-1}[t]\, \rv[t]$, each coordinate satisfies the trust-region constraint $\E \{ \Delta^{2}[t+1,i] \} \le \gain{\mu}^2$, and therefore it follows that overall at each iteration, the satisfied trust-region radius is . \[\E\{\|\Delta[t+1]\|_2^2\} \le n\,\gain{\mu}^2.\] Realization . A simple but practical way to realize this scaling chocie is via an exponential moving average (EMA) . \[\begin{aligned} \rb[t,i] &= \rho\,\rb[t-1,i] + (1-\rho)\,\rg^{2}[t,i], \end{aligned}\] where $\rho$ is a positive constant, close to but less than $1$. Additional refinements are possible. For example, dividing its output by $1-\rho^{t}$ removes the transient bias induced by initialization of the EMA recursion. Finally, . \[\begin{aligned} \lambda^{-1}[t,i] = \Bigl(\sqrt{\rb[t,i]} + \eps\Bigr)^{-1}, \end{aligned}\] where $\eps$ is a small positive constant used to artificially ensure numerical stable computation of the reciprocal of $\sqrt{\rb[t,i]}$. The only moment statistic attempted to be estimated here is the per-coordinate gradient’s second moment. Summary . The scaling choice in the learning-rate and its realization above performs coordinate-wise normalization, and corresponds to Adam (or RMSProp if $\gamma=\beta=0$) and we see that it directly enforces a per-coordinate trust-region constraint . \[\E\{\Delta^{2}[t+1,i]\} \le \gain{\mu}^2,\] on the energy of the parameter step (update increments) controlled by a constant $\gain{\mu}$. Muon (Matrix-valued Operator-level Formulation) . We now extend the parameter update form to a two-dimensional matrix operator-level update form. This operator-level formulation replaces scalar learning rates with linear operators acting on the gradient and replaces scalar second moments with covariance operators. Throughout, we use the Frobenius inner product . \[\langle A,B\rangle_F = \Tr(A^\intercal B), \quad \text{and}\quad \|A\|_F^2 = \langle A,A\rangle_F,\] for any matrix $A,B\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times m}$, where $1 < n \le m < \infty$. Let $\rw[t],\;\rg[t],\;\rv[t],\;\Delta[t+1]\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times m}$, and let the learning rate operator $\gain{\alpha[t]}\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}$ be symmetric and positive definite, such that $\gain{\alpha[t]}=\gain{\alpha[t]}^\intercal$. Scaling Choice . For this formulation, we will choose the scaling rule for normalization in the parameter update step as . \[\begin{aligned} \lambda[t] = C^{\frac{1}{2}}[t],\quad \text{where} \quad C[t] &= {\rv[t]\,\rv[t]^\intercal}, \end{aligned}\] The scaling rule is preserved as the matrix square root of a symmetric autocorrelation matrix of the filtered gradient. The inverse scale $\lambda^{-1}[t]=C^{-\frac{1}{2}}[t]$, the square root of the inverse of a symmetric positive-definite matrix $C[t]$. Therefore, this directly involves estimation of the inverse of the square-root of a matrix. Trust-region Constraint . Consequently, $|\Delta[t+1]|_F^2 = \gain{\mu}^2 \,|C^{-\frac{1}{2}}[t]\,\rv[t]|_F^2 $, and since $|C^{-\frac{1}{2}}[t]\,\rv[t]|_F^2 = \operatorname{tr}(I_n)$, where $I_n\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}$ is an identity matrix, it follows that . \[\|\Delta[t+1]\|_F^2 = n\,\gain{\mu}^2,\] which implies that the scaling choice can be interpreted as satisfying a constant trust-region constraint per iteration similar to the coordinate-wise scaling rule. The coordinate-wise scaling rule enforces the trust-region constraint by measuring the expected Euclidean-norm as the energy of the parameter update step. In contrast, the matrix-valued scaling rule directly imposes a row-wise trust-region constraint measured in the Frobenius-norm. In both cases, the maximum possible trust-region radius is explicitly controlled by a scalar constant $\gain{\mu}$, acting per coordinate as previously seen, and per row for this matrix-valued formulation. Implementation . A practical way to realize this matrix inverse square-root normalization is via the efficient polynomial method introduced by Lakić (Lakić, 1998). This method provides a family of stable, rapidly convergent recursions parameterized by a polynomial of order $j$, and includes several classical schemes as special cases. During implementation, invertibility of $C[t]$ can be artificially ensured by adding a scaled identity matrix $\epsilon\,I_n$ to $C[t]$. Let $D[0] = I_n$, and $S[0] = u^{-1}\,C[t]\,D^2[0]$, where $u=|C[t]|_F$, and $u^{-1}\,C[t]$ is a normalized form of $C[t]$. The matrix inverse square-root recurrence from $k=1,\ldots,K$ is \(\begin{aligned} D[k+1] &= D[k]\,\varphi[k] \\ S[k+1] &= S[k]\,\varphi^2[k], \end{aligned}\) . where $\varphi[k]$ is a degree $j-1$ polynomial in $S[k]$ and $S[k] = u^{-1}C[t]\rmD^2[k]$. For the lowest polynomial order $j\ge2$ the corresponding $\varphi[k]$ is . \[\begin{aligned} j=2,\quad& \varphi[k] = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl(3\,I_n - S[k]\bigr).\\ j=3,\quad& \varphi[k] = \tfrac{1}{8}\bigl(15\,I_n - 10\,S[k] + 3S^2[k]\bigr)\\ j=4,\quad& \varphi[k] = \tfrac{1}{16}\bigl(35\,I_n - 35\,S[k] + 21\,S^2[k] - 5\,S^3[k]\bigr)\\ j=5,\quad& \varphi[k] = \tfrac{1}{128}\bigl(315\,I_n - 420\,S[k] + 378\,S^2[k] - 180\,S^3[k] + 35\,S^4[k] \bigr). \end{aligned}\] This iterative scheme converges with Q-order $j$, and for low $j\in{3, 4, 5}$, a few iterations ($K \ge 3$) is typically sufficient. As ${k\to\infty}$, then the recurrence converges: $D[k]\to u^{\frac{1}{2}}C[t]^{-\frac{1}{2}}$, and $S[k]\to I_n$. Notably, the classical Newton-Schulz iteration in (Higham, 2008) can be recovered as the special case $j=2$ of Lakić’s polynomial family. Muon (Momentum Orthogonalized by Newton-Schulz) (Large et al., 2024; Bernstein & Newhouse, 2024) can be recovered by the single implementation of $\Delta[t+1] = -\gain{\mu}\,\lambda^{-1}[t]\, \rv[t] = -\gain{\mu}\,\bar{\rv}[t]$. Using a reduced singular value decomposition $\rv[t]=U[t]\Sigma[t]{V^\intercal}[t]$, the normalized filtered gradient $\bar{\rv}[t]=C^{-\frac{1}{2}}[t]\,\rv[t]=U[t]{V^\intercal}[t]$ can also be efficiently realized via a Newton-Schulz recurrence or similar higher-order polynomial recurrences. Unified Insight . Both algorithms (Adam or Muon) follow a general update form: . \[\Delta[t+1] = \gain{\mu} \cdot \lambda^{-1}[t] \cdot \rv[t]\] The only difference is how \(\lambda\) is defined. New optimizers can be designed explicitly by (i) designing a gradient filter and (ii) choosing a learning-rate rule that encodes gradient normalization via a trust-region principle.

35.title: Conclusion: Your New Perspective on Learning and Optimizers
35.content: Instead of relying on physical metaphors, we can: . | Treat gradients as signals | Design filters explicitly | Treat the learning-rate as a trust-region object | Design trust-region optimal principles | . You have likely moved beyond seeing AI training as a black box of weights and biases. You now understand it as a sophisticated signal processing task. Momentum is the heartbeat of this process, a digital filter that helps to separate the signal from the noise of the data. 3-Point Takeaway . | Momentum is a Filter It is a first-order lowpass filter designed to attenuate high-frequency noise and preserve the underlying signal. | Poles and Zeros are the Design Knobs The difference between Heavy-Ball and Nesterov is determined by: \(\filter{\gamma}\) . | Stability is the True Goal Momentum expands the stability region, allowing aggressive learning rates while maintaining safe updates within a trust region radius: \(\gain{\mu}\) . | . We will show in Part 3 that the optimal $\gain{\mu}$ that minimizes the mean-squared error dynamics of the stochastic error setup laid out in Part 1: Momentum as Lowpass Filter Design changes as a result of the kurtosis $\kappa$, tail-heaviness of the stochastic process. Open Question . If popular algorithms encode this modular principle, then: What unexplored filtering plus trust-region design principles could unlock the next generation of optimizers? . | Polyak, B.
